perturbation.original_text: Because of the trade secret subject matter of Entera's business, Disclosee agrees that it will not solicit the services of any of the employees of Entera during the term of this Agreement and for ninety (90) days thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: Disclosee agrees that it will not solicit the services of any of the employees of Entera during the term of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 90-day restriction on soliciting employees after the agreement's termination could violate California law regarding non-solicitation agreements. California law requires these agreements to be narrowly tailored.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. While non-solicitation agreements are sometimes permitted if narrowly tailored, removing the temporal limitation ('90 days thereafter') makes the restriction overly broad and potentially unenforceable under California law, thus contradicting the intent and limitations of permissible restrictions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text removes the 90-day restriction on soliciting employees after the agreement terminates, which creates a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that removing this temporal limitation makes the restriction overly broad and potentially unenforceable under California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600 (a), states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' The removal of the 90-day limit in the changed text potentially broadens the restriction beyond what is legally permissible, creating a contradiction with California law's restrictions on non-compete agreements. The perturbation explanation further indicates this omission could violate California law regarding narrowly tailored non-solicitation agreements.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that the unauthorized disclosure or use by the Receiving Party of the Disclosing Party's proprietary and confidential Information will cause irreparable harm and significant injury, the degree of which may be difficult to ascertain. Accordingly, each Party agrees that the Disclosing Party will have the right to obtain an immediate injunction enjoining any breach of this Agreement, as well as the right to pursue any and all other rights and remedies available at law or in equity for such a breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that the unauthorized disclosure or use by the Receiving Party of the Disclosing Party's proprietary and confidential Information may cause harm. Accordingly, each Party agrees that the Disclosing Party will have the right to pursue remedies available at law for such a breach.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the mention of 'irreparable harm' and the right to an 'immediate injunction', the contract makes it harder for the disclosing party to obtain urgent legal protection for trade secret misappropriation, potentially violating the Defend Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) allows for injunctive relief in cases of trade secret misappropriation. By weakening the acknowledgment of potential harm and removing the explicit right to an injunction, the contract undermines the availability of remedies provided by the DTSA for trade secret violations, making it more difficult to obtain necessary and urgent legal protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures the disclosing party has the 'right to obtain an immediate injunction'. By removing the mention of 'irreparable harm' and the right to an 'immediate injunction', the changed contract weakens the legal protection for trade secret misappropriation. The Law Explanation states that the DTSA allows for injunctive relief in cases of trade secret misappropriation, and the contract changes undermine the availability of remedies provided by the DTSA.

perturbation.original_text: To be covered hereunder, Information disclosed in writing must be marked "proprietary", "confidential", or by words of similar import. Information disclosed orally or visually and identified at the time as proprietary or confidential shall be covered hereunder if it is reduced to writing, marked as provided herein, and transmitted to Receiving Party within thirty (30) days after disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: To be covered hereunder, Information disclosed in writing must be marked "proprietary", "confidential", or by words of similar import. Information disclosed orally or visually and identified at the time as proprietary or confidential shall be covered hereunder regardless if it is reduced to writing.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to reduce orally disclosed confidential information to writing within 30 days could make it unenforceable under California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), California Civil Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Removing the 30-day writing requirement for oral disclosures eliminates a reasonable measure to protect confidentiality, potentially rendering the information ineligible for trade secret protection under CUTSA, and creating a legal risk of unenforceability.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The omission of the 30-day writing requirement for oral disclosures, as described in the 'Perturbation Explanation' and 'Law Explanation', directly contradicts the principle of 'reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets' under California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the relevant section of the California Civil Code (CIV 3426) as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Removing the requirement to document oral disclosures within 30 days eliminates a concrete measure for protecting confidentiality, thus creating a significant risk of unenforceability under CUTSA because it weakens efforts to maintain secrecy. This is a direct contradiction rather than a minor weakening because it removes a specific action previously required to protect the trade secret.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to Article 3 hereof, each Party receiving Information under the Agreement shall hold Information in confidence in accordance with the terms of the Agreement for a period of two (2) years from the date of receipt thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to Article 3 hereof, each Party receiving Information under the Agreement shall hold Information in confidence in accordance with the terms of the Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the two-year limitation on confidentiality could be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under California law, particularly if the information is not a true trade secret or if the duration is excessively long for the type of information disclosed.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1670.4 suggests that non-disclosure agreements for an indefinite period could be deemed unreasonable. By removing the two-year limitation, the agreement becomes open-ended, potentially contradicting California law which looks unfavorably upon overly broad or perpetual confidentiality obligations, especially if they restrain trade unreasonably.

perturbation.justification: The original contract limited the confidentiality period to two years, while the changed contract removes this limitation, making it indefinite. The Law Explanation states that California Civil Code § 1670.4 suggests that non-disclosure agreements for an indefinite period could be deemed unreasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references California Code, CIV 1670.4. The omission of the time limit in the changed text creates a potential contradiction with California law, as overly broad or perpetual confidentiality obligations are not favored, making the duration potentially unreasonable and unenforceable. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition, specifically when a contract removes essential information, which in this case is the duration of the confidentiality agreement.
